title: Headache & Intracranial Pressure: A Systematic Approach
objectives: Understand the pathophysiology of Headache & Intracranial Pressure
Identify key diagnostic features of Headache & Intracranial Pressure
Apply clinical reasoning to Headache & Intracranial Pressure management
Recognize common pitfalls in Headache & Intracranial Pressure

sections.title: Overview of Headache & Intracranial Pressure
sections.content:
**Headache & Intracranial Pressure**

Idiopathic intracranial hypertension, secondary causes of elevated ICP, papilledema, LP opening pressure

**Key Points:**
• IIH (pseudotumor cerebri): elevated ICP without structural lesion, normal CSF composition
• Classic patient: young obese woman; Symptoms: headache, transient visual obscurations, pulsatile tinnitus, diplopia (CN VI palsy)
• Papilledema on fundoscopy (bilateral disc edema)

sections.title: Pathophysiology & Key Features
sections.content:
• IIH (pseudotumor cerebri): elevated ICP without structural lesion, normal CSF composition
• Classic patient: young obese woman; Symptoms: headache, transient visual obscurations, pulsatile tinnitus, diplopia (CN VI palsy)

sections.title: Diagnosis & Management
sections.content:
• Papilledema on fundoscopy (bilateral disc edema)
• LP: opening pressure >25 cmH2O with normal CSF composition
• Treatment: weight loss, acetazolamide (reduces CSF production); optic nerve sheath fenestration or VP shunt if vision threatened

sections.title: Clinical Pearls
sections.content:
**High-Yield Points for Headache & Intracranial Pressure:**

• Papilledema on fundoscopy (bilateral disc edema)
• LP: opening pressure >25 cmH2O with normal CSF composition
• Treatment: weight loss, acetazolamide (reduces CSF production); optic nerve sheath fenestration or VP shunt if vision threatened

options.text: IIH (pseudotumor cerebri): elevated ICP without structural lesion, normal CSF composition
options.explanation: IIH (pseudotumor cerebri): elevated ICP without structural lesion, normal CSF composition

options.text: Requires invasive testing in all cases

options.explanation: Treatment: weight loss, acetazolamide (reduces CSF production); optic nerve sheath fenestration or VP shunt if vision threatened

options.text: Immediate surgical intervention in all cases

mcqs.question: A patient presents with findings consistent with Headache & Intracranial Pressure. Which finding would you expect?

options.text: Classic patient: young obese woman; Symptoms: headache, transient visual obscurations, pulsatile tinnitus, diplopia (CN VI palsy)
options.explanation: Classic patient: young obese woman; Symptoms: headache, transient visual obscurations, pulsatile tinnitus, diplopia (CN VI palsy)

mcqs.stem: A 30-year-old woman presents with optic neuritis. MRI brain shows 4 periventricular white matter lesions. CSF shows oligoclonal bands. Which criteria are met for MS diagnosis?

options.text: Dissemination in space (DIS) is met; need evidence of dissemination in time (DIT) for definitive diagnosis
options.explanation: Per the 2017 McDonald criteria, DIS requires ≥1 T2 lesion in ≥2 of 4 MS-typical regions (periventricular, cortical/juxtacortical, infratentorial, spinal cord). With periventricular lesions + optic neuritis (separate CNS region), DIS is met. DIT can be met by a new T2 or gadolinium-enhancing lesion on follow-up MRI, or simultaneously by both enhancing and non-enhancing lesions.

options.text: Both DIS and DIT are met — diagnose MS now
options.explanation: DIS is met but DIT requires evidence of lesions at different time points. The initial scan alone (even with oligoclonal bands) cannot establish DIT unless both enhancing AND non-enhancing lesions are present simultaneously.

options.text: Neither criterion is met — optic neuritis is a clinically isolated syndrome only
options.explanation: While optic neuritis alone is a CIS, the 4 periventricular white matter lesions establish DIS. CIS + DIS constitutes significant risk for MS.

options.text: CSF oligoclonal bands alone establish the diagnosis regardless of MRI findings
options.explanation: Oligoclonal bands support the diagnosis and can substitute for DIT in certain scenarios per 2017 McDonald criteria, but they do not independently establish the diagnosis without meeting DIS.
